IDirectSoundStream::FlushEx

Flushes the stream (stops output and completes all packets) either synchronously or asynchronously.

HRESULT FlushEx(
    REFERENCE_TIME rtTimeStamp,
    DWORD dwFlags
);

Parameters

rtTimeStamp
REFERENCE_TIME value specifying the time at which the flush operation should be performed. Specify zero to initiate the flush operation immediately.
dwFlags
Value that contains the flags that indicate how the flush should be performed. Can be a combination of the following values:
Value Description
DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_IMMEDIATE The flush operation should be performed immediately in a synchronous operation. When FlushEx returns, the flush will have been completed. Calling FlushEx with this value is identical to calling Flush, and may cause a busy-wait state (see below).

The rtTimeStamp parameter must be set to zero when this flag is specified.
DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ASYNC The flush operation should be performed as an asynchronous operation. If rtTimeStamp is nonzero and specifies a time in the future, FlushEx will post the flush request (to be performed during a future call to DirectSoundDoWork) and return immediately. Otherwise, FlushEx will initiate the flush operation and return without waiting for the flush operation to complete.

Use the standard packet completion mechanisms or IDirectSoundStream::GetStatus to determine when the flush has completed (see the example below).
DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ENVELOPE The stream should enter the release portion of its envelope before performing the flush.

The DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ASYNC flag must be set when using this flag.
DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ENVELOPE2 When the release segment completes, status returned by IDirectSoundStream::GetStatus will include DSSTREAMSTATUS_ENVELOPECOMPLETE. This bit will remain set until the stream is flushed again with a call to either Flush or FlushEx).

The DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ASYNC flag must be set when using this flag.

Return Values

Returns S_OK if successful, an error code otherwise.

Remarks

FlushEx allows titles to flush a stream synchronously or post an asynchronous flush request. Flushing a stream is similar to calling IDirectSoundBuffer8::Stop on a DirectSound buffer.

Because of restrictions in the hardware, using the DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_IMMEDIATE flag to perform a synchronous flush may cause a busy-wait state while waiting for the hardware voice to stop. Calling FlushEx with DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_IMMEDIATE is equivalent to calling Flush.

To determine when an asynchronous flush has completed, either use standard packet completion mechanisms or poll GetStatus and check for DSSTREAMSTATUS_PLAYING, as shown in the following example:

DWORD dwStatus;
pDSStream->FlushEx( 0, DSSTREAMFLUSHEX_ASYNC );
do
{
    // Perform other tasks while waiting for FlushEx to complete
	
    pDSStream->GetStatus( &dwStatus );
} while( dwStatus & DSSTREAMSTATUS_PLAYING );

Do not perform additional operations on the stream until the flush has completed. Calling some methods on the stream immediately after flushing the stream can generate debug warning messages. See DirectSound Warning Messages for more information.

Packet completion notifications can be enabled by specifying a callback function in the lpfnCallback member of the DSSTREAMDESC structure when the stream is created with DirectSoundCreateStream.

IDirectSound8::GetTime returns the current reference time, which can be added to in order to specify a future reference time for rtTimeStamp.

Requirements

Header: Declared in DSound.h.

Import Library: Use DSound.lib.

See Also

IDirectSoundStream::Flush, IDirectSoundStream::GetStatus, IDirectSound8::GetTime, DirectSoundDoWork, DSSTREAMDESC, DirectSoundCreateStream
